name: fixture-04-supply-chain-director-manufacturing
description: Senior Supply Chain Director at a tier-1 auto supplier moving to a medical device manufacturer. Strong operational experience but the medical device domain has different regulatory pressure (FDA QSR, GMP).

resume_data.name: Robert Akinwale
key_skills: Supply chain risk + resilience (dual-source, inventory buffer modeling)
Lean Six Sigma Black Belt — cost-to-serve + waste reduction
key_achievements: Re-engineered the supplier base during the semiconductor shortage — cut 14-day supply risk from 38% of the plant's SKUs to 4% over 11 months without inventory bloat.
Led the S&OP redesign for the $1.4B North American business — cut forecast-to-production latency from 9 weeks to 3 while improving fill rate 94.2% → 98.7%.
Managed the $280M sourcing portfolio across 340 suppliers; negotiated 4.1% aggregate cost reduction in a 6%-inflation environment.

requirements: 15+ years of supply chain leadership in a regulated manufacturing environment
Demonstrated track record of cost discipline in a complex supplier network
Experience with FDA 21 CFR 820 (Quality System Regulation) and ISO 13485
Strong command of S&OP and demand planning at enterprise scale
Executive-level communication with commercial, regulatory, and quality partners
culture_cues: matrix reporting to the COO and Chief Quality Officer

letter_plan.opening_hook: Lead with the semiconductor-shortage resilience work — the exact kind of crisis-management supply chain instinct Crescent needs — framed to signal that resilience thinking, not cost-only thinking, is the mental model.
body_points: Semiconductor risk reduction (38% → 4%) demonstrates the supplier-resilience muscle medical device supply chains demand — patient-supply continuity is the medical-device analog to auto OEM line-stoppage, so the playbook transfers.

S&OP redesign + fill-rate improvement shows the enterprise-scale demand-planning depth the JD flags; mention Lean Six Sigma Black Belt as the methodological foundation for the quality-before-speed culture.

$280M portfolio + cost-reduction-in-inflation proves the cost-discipline track record. Address the FDA QSR / ISO 13485 gap directly — auto has IATF 16949 + PPAP, a parallel but distinct control set, so the regulatory mindset transfers but the specific framework learning is honest.
letter_plan.closing_strategy: Acknowledge the regulatory learning curve as the honest gap; close on the resilience-first instinct and the track record of operating inside a 'stoppage is existential' mindset — the cultural frame patient-first medical device work requires.
